turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has the opinion of the Court in case 18-489, Taggart v. Lorenzen.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: At the end of a bankruptcy case, the bankruptcy court normally enters a discharge order. That means the debtor is discharged from most of his pre-bankruptcy debts. Now, it has long been the case that the bankruptcy code can authorize a bankruptcy court to hold in civil contempt a person who tries to collect a debt that that discharge order says he does not have to pay. And the question here concerns what are the criteria for holding somebody who tries to do that, they say they are not trying to do it, the other side says they are trying to do it, and the question is, what are the criteria for holding them in civil contempt? The bankruptcy court said well, this is the matter sort of like strict liability. You can hold that person in civil contempt, no matter what he thought if he happened to be aware of the discharge order and wanted to take the actions that turn out to violate it. So the creditors here he said are in civil contempt. The Ninth Circuit held about the opposite. It applied a subjective standard. It said, no court, you cannot hold this person in civil contempt as long as that person had a good faith belief that the discharge order did not apply to what he was trying to do. And that is so even if the creditor's belief, the belief that he could go collect this money, was completely unreasonable. All right, so we have two opposite extremes. So we reject both of them. And what we do is we think that a court can hold a creditor in civil contempt for violating a discharge order, trying to collect something that the discharge order says you cannot, if there is not a fair ground of doubt as to whether the order bars the creditor's conduct; in other words, if there is a fair ground of doubt. Does this order stop you from doing what you are trying to do creditor, then you cannot try to collect it. Civil contempt may be appropriate if there is not an objectively reasonable basis for concluding that the creditor's conduct might be lawful. And where do we get this standard from, other than the briefs, is a longstanding interpretive principle. A statutory term transplanted from another legal source brings the old soil with it. Here the bankruptcy code specifies that a bankruptcy discharge order is like an injunction and a court can issue any order or judgment necessary to carry it out. Now, those suggest an old soil, because you go and look and see how the court normally applies injunctions. In cases outside of bankruptcy, we have said that civil contempt is not appropriate where there is a fair ground of doubt as to the wrongfulness of the defendant's conduct and that is normally an objective standard. And lo and behold, we transplant that to this new place and we say the party's subjective belief that he or she was complying with an order ordinarily does not insulate her from a finding of civil contempt. The subjective standard applied by the Ninth Circuit is inconsistent with this traditional principle, so too is the strict liability standard that was adopted by the bankruptcy court and that the petitioner Taggart was pressing in this case. For these reasons which are discussed at greater length in our opinion, we vacate the judgment of the Ninth Circuit and we remand the case to apply the standard set forth in the opinion, which is unanimous.
